pullRequest.title: Add drawing bitmap images to Canvas
pullRequest.body: This adds a `draw_image` method to the `Canvas` class, along with a `DrawImage` subclass of `DrawingObject` that adds the ability to render an `Image` into `Canvas` with the current transforms applied. It currently supports the ability to draw an image at a point, or to draw an image into a destination rectangle, but doesn't currently support the ability to draw a slice out of the source image. Has tests, and have added image drawing as one of the options in the Canvas example. Fixes #955. pullRequest.title: Fix mid-path transforms
Don't clear path after fill() or stroke().
pullRequest.body:
This brings the behaviour of the Canvas context into alignment with the way that the HTML Canvas handles stroking and filling and paths (as compared to the behaviour of CoreGraphics contexts).

The [Qt Canvas PR](#3992) should already pass the tests in the PR with no further modification.

The HTML canvas `fillText`/`strokeText` don't affect the current path, so `write_text` for now should have similar behaviour: text is put into a separate path and rendered without changing the current path. See [Mozilla's docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/CanvasRenderingContext2D/fillText):
> This method draws directly to the canvas without modifying the current path, so any subsequent [fill()](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/CanvasRenderingContext2D/fill) or [stroke()](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/CanvasRenderingContext2D/stroke) calls will have no effect on it.

Fixes #2207.

pullRequest.title: Fix OptionContainer layout behaviors on Cocoa and GTK
pullRequest.body:
<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->

Fixes #2288 along with other behaviors on Cocoa.  All 3 of these are kinda coupled.

The first issue here is that content_refreshed sets minimum sizes of the containers, and rehint uses these minimum sizes.  However rehint comes before content_refreshed, so in content_refreshed we add a check if the intrinsic size changes, and rerefresh if it changes.

A similar fix is done in GTK backend as well in this PR, since Qt is about to land soon, and the GTK mechanism *almost* works, and I didn't want to add a "skip_on_backend(gtk)" flag just to save me from a few lines of extra changes here.

The second issue is that rehinting does not take proper account into the decorations of the OptionContainer.  We account for the decors by taking the difference betwene the alignment rect (which is what Auto Layout is bsaed on; excluding shadows and ornaments) and the content size.  The reliance of native sizing is not a huge concern here, since the code there will work out for any nonzero frame, so even if the layout of the widget is incorrect at that time, the hinting will work.

The last issue is #2288.  I've identified a nicer and more general way to fix the issue than what I described at #2288 -- handling any situation when applySplit is called with 0 size, which means there should be way fewer issues with applying split with invalid sizes now.  There's a comment inline about it -- since I was touching rehinting logic in OptionContainer, I originally planned to fix this as described in #2288 but still relayouting nonvisible containers (instead of doing nothing, since that'll give incorrect hinting); but that'd need me manually calling container_refreshed, and does not feel as elegant as a special check for 0 size when applying split.

I've also replaced my previously added ``self.interface.window._impl.native.layoutIfNeeded()`` with ``self.native.layoutSubtreeIfNeeded()`` as that's a more elegant way to write it, and also fixes side effects of #3929's root cause.

pullRequest.title: iOS widgets in scroll containers now touchable below the fold
pullRequest.body:
## Summary
Fixes #2411 - iOS ScrollContainer touch event handling for buttons initially rendered off-screen.

## Problem
Buttons in ScrollContainers that were initially off-screen didn't respond to touch events when scrolled into view on iOS.

## Root Cause
The document container's frame wasn't being updated when the scroll view's contentSize expanded, causing iOS hit testing to fail for views outside the  original container bounds.

## Solution
Update the document container frame to match the content size in `content_refreshed()`:
```python
self.document_container.native.frame = CGRectMake(0, 0, width, height)
```
Testing

- Manually tested all buttons 0-19 from the test program in <https://github.com/beeware/toga/issues/2411> respond correctly in iOS Simulator
- Verified initially visible buttons still work (regression test)
- Confirmed fix works for all buttons scrolled into view

Impact

- Fixes the specific issue without affecting other functionality
- Maintains backward compatibility
- Uses standard iOS patterns for container frame management

Fixes <https://github.com/beeware/toga/issues/2411>

pullRequest.title: Lazy loading of implementations via entry points
pullRequest.body:
PR implements the ideas discussed in #2687 using entry points to contribute widget implementations so that they can be lazily loaded at the point of need, rather than having a monolithic `factory` module which loads the entire collection of backend implementations at once.

The API is a new `get_factory(implementation)` function which is intended as a replacement either returns:
- a `Factory` object which lazily loads attributes via entrypoints in the a group of the form `{implementation}.backend.{backend}` (where `implementation` is `"toga_core"` for now, but could be `"togax_..."` for projects which want to contribute new widgets)
- a `factory` module loaded in the old way for backwards compatibility

The `factory` attribute on a `Widget` is now a property which defaults to the `toga_core` factory, but `Widget` subclasses implementing new widgets should override and instantiate a factory that points at an appropriate `togax_*` entry point group.

It includes an example of a simple widget and a topic guide on extending.  All of this makes it a fairly large addition.

There is a sketch implementation of a moderately complex new widget here https://github.com/corranwebster/toga_bitmap_view.  It all seems to work pretty well. The only awkward thing is when adding new entry points you need to do a `pip install -e .` on the project so it picks up the new metadata.

It should all be backwards compatible to some extent, and so I haven't bumped the version number, but this may be a big enough architectural change to become 0.6.0.

Fixes #2687.
Fixes #2928.

pullRequest.title: bugfix: Use locale in native_decimal for string conversion
pullRequest.body:
Updated native_decimal function to use `locale.str()` for string conversion.

<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
Set `locale` to the region used by the system with `locale.setlocale(locale.LC_ALL, '')`.
Replaced `str(value)` with `locale.str(value)`, so that the string in the conversion to decimal will use the same decimal separator as the OS.

<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
In case of the mismatch between the system's language and the language set as the default in Python, using `toga.NumberInput` could cause an issue for languages using a comma for the decimal separator. 

Example:
`toga.NumberInput(min=0, max=10, step=0.1, value=0)`  would result with a `System.FormatException` in `System.Convert.ToDecimal(String value)`

<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->
Closes [#2773](https://github.com/beeware/toga/issues/2773)

pullRequest.title: Allow the use of .NET Core 10
pullRequest.body:
Allows the Winforms backend to use .NET Core 10, instead of .NET Framework 4.x.

This is required to add native Windows on ARM support - Windows 10 and 11 both provide .NET Framework 4.x (aka netfx) out of the box, but on ARM machines, it's an x86-64 build. 

This adds in a loader configuration that will try to use .NET Core 10; if .NET 10 can't be found, it will try to fall back on .NET Framework 4.x. When running on ARM with a native ARM Python interpreter, this fallback will fail, so an error is raised.

In addition to the .NET loading changes, the changes in this PR are:

* Changes in class names for status icons
* Windows not accurately restoring the window size when returning from FULLSCREEN or PRESENTATION.
* Fixes to the testbed due to slightly different behavior in folder selection dialogs
* The need for an ARM binary for WebView2 (and related changes to packaging so we have a `py3-none-win_arm64` and `py3-none-win_amd64` binary wheels.

With the changes in this PR, the testbed passes to completion in my test environment with a small number of errors related to discrepancies caused by the virtualisation environment (discrepancies between `\\drive\location` and `z:\location` path descriptions). 

However, the testbed *doesn't* pass using the `windows-11-arm` environment, as that runner is configured as a non-interactive/headless environment. This means that no window ever gains focus, and we have no ability to test focus changes, dialog display, etc. See actions/partner-runner-images#174 for details.

So - this PR does a full CI pass on both .NET Framework 4.x and .NET Core 10 on Windows/x86-64, validating that the .NET Core APIs work; but only does a basic "does the app start?" test for Windows/ARM64.

Two design decisions implicit in this PR:
* We are continuing to support .NET Framework 4.x. This is available by default on Windows 10 and 11, so it means all Toga apps will Just Work. The .NET Desktop runtime is widely installed, but isn't *guaranteed* to be installed. It would definitely be *easier* to only support .NET 10, but I'm not sure that's a great user experience. We can add a "this app requires .NET 10" to an MSI installer - but we can't easily bundle .NET 10 *with* an MSI installer (at least, not in a way that yields a single simple MSI).
* We will continuing to support Windows 10. Win10 is strictly EOL, so we *could* drop it. If we did, I think we can go back to a pure Python Winforms wheel. The binary wheel is only needed because Windows 10 doesn't include the WebView2 runtime;0 Windows 11 does. If we drop Windows 10 support, we could simplify that part of the code as well.

However, my read is that Windows users are the *least* likely to be on the "bleeding edge" when it comes to new tech - we still get requests for restoring Windows 7/8 support - so dropping Windows 10 (even though it *is* EOL) is probably not a good idea quite yet.

Support for Windows/ARM64 has been merged into Briefcase; I've manually published stub binaries for Python 3.12 so that `briefcase run` works. See beeware/briefcase-windows-VisualStudio-template#89 for some template tweaks that are needed to tag those binaries.

Fixes #2782.

pullRequest.title: Toga examples syntax cleanup and consistency update
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3138
Fixes #3624 

## Functional updates

* `.template` directory and example cookiecutter removed per @freakboy3742.
* ImageView now demonstrates passing image path directly to `ImageView`, simplifies Pillow demo, and makes PIL an optional install so the app will run without it present. (Per discussion with @HalfWhitt)
* ProgressBar `+`/`-` buttons are swapped into the more logical order of `-`/`+` based on the direction the progress bar moves when clicking each of the buttons, and includes the improvements discussed in #3624.
* Screenshot no longer uses deprecated `add_background_task()`.

## Style syntax updates

Updates examples to remove `style=Pack`, and/or to import `COLUMN` etc. from `toga.constants`. 

Per @HalfWhitt, where `style = Pack(foo=1)` followed by `style=style` usage is updated, the `Pack()` object is replaced with a dict (e.g. `{"foo": 1}`), and `style=` is replaced with `**` (e.g. `**style`). 

Examples with the above updates:
* activityindicator
* beeliza
* box
* button
* canvas
* colors
* command
* date_and_time
* detailedlist
* dialogs
* divider
* documentapp
* examples_overview
* focus
* font
* font_size
* handlers
* hardware
* imageview
* layout
* mapview
* multilinetextinput
* numberinput
* optioncontainer
* passwordinput
* progressbar
* resize TODO: Verify running properly
* screenshot
* scrollcontainer
* selection
* simpleapp
* slider
* splitcontainer
* statusiconapp
* switch_demo
* table
* table_source
* textinput
* tree
* tree_source
* Tutorials 1-4
* webview
* window

## Updates for consistency across example apps

* In `if __name__ == "__main__":` - `app = main() ... app.mainloop()` simplified to `main().main_loop()`
* In `def main():` - `app = AppName(...) ... return app` simplified to `return AppName(...)`
* App class renamed to `WidgetNameApp` from `ExampleWidgetNameApp`, in the following examples, as discussed with @HalfWhitt. I chose to go with the `WidgetNameApp` convention because the wording in the READMEs ("Test app for the Widget Name Example widget") was confusing, and some of the class names were unnecessarily long.
  * activityindicator
  * box
  * button
  * canvas
  * command
  * detailedlist
  * dialogs
  * divider
  * documentapp
  * examples_overview
  * focus
  * font
  * handlers
  * hardware
  * layout
  * mapview
  * multilinetextinput
  * numberinput
  * optioncontainer
  * simpleapp
  * statusiconapp
  * table
  * table_source
  * tree
  * tree_source
  * webview
  * window

## README updates

This is less relevant because the READMEs will all show up as new, but I'm leaving this in for posterity.

* Converts all READMEs from reStructuredText to Markdown, per offer from @freakboy3742. (This is in preparation for updating the example overview app to fully render the README files, a change which I will be deferring to a subsequent PR.)
* Adds relevant documentation links to all READMEs with direct docs links, otherwise links to Toga docs.
* Updates `pip install` in all README files to `python -m pip install`.
* Adds `python -m pip install toga` to all READMEs where it was not present.
* Box README no longer refers to "example".
* Button README updated to reflect current style engine, title no longer refers to "example".
* Date and Time README reflects macOS support, no longer refers to "example".
* Divider README no longer references "switch".
* DocumentApp README now includes note about macOS behavior when run without a command line argument.
* Font README no longer refers to "example".
* Handlers README no longer duplicate of Dialogs' README.
* ImageView README now reflects current example features.
* NumberInput README no longer refers to "demo".
* OptionContainer README no longer refers to "example".
* ProgressBar README title no longer includes "example".
* Screenshot README Quick Start now refers to using it with Briefcase.
* Selection README no longer duplicate of Switch's README.
* Slider README updated to reflect current style engine, no longer contains a reference to "switch", and title no longer includes "example".
* Tree Source README typo fixed.

pullRequest.title: Added slider widget to core and cocoa.
pullRequest.body:
I'm slowly building all the needed widgets for the Settings API, here comes the slider :)
Should the slider also have a label? By default cocoa and iOS don't have one.


Test Program:
~~~python
import time
import toga
from colosseum import CSS


def build(app):
    def callback(widget):
        label.text = '{0:.2f}'.format(widget.value)

    def btn_callback(widget):
        slider.enabled = True if slider.enabled is False else False

    def btn_set_to(widget):
        slider.value = 5

    slider = toga.Slider(range=(0, 10), on_slide=callback)
    slider.style = CSS(width=200)

    label = toga.Label(slider.value, style=CSS(flex=1))

    box_label_slider = toga.Box(children=[slider, label])
    box_label_slider.style = CSS(margin=20, flex_direction='row', justify_content='center', align_items='center')

    button = toga.Button('enable/disable', on_press=btn_callback, style=CSS(flex=1))
    btn_enable = toga.Button('set to 5', on_press=btn_set_to, style=CSS(flex=1))

    style = CSS(flex=1, padding=30)
    box = toga.Box(style=style)

    box.add(box_label_slider)
    box.add(button)
    box.add(btn_enable)
    return box


if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = toga.App('Test Slider', 'org.pybee.helloworld', startup=build)
    app.main_loop()

~~~

Signed-off-by: Jonas Schell <jonasschell@ocupe.org>

pullRequest.title: Fixing MultilineTextInput on_change Event Firing on WIndows
pullRequest.body:
<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->
The content based solution to make sure the MultilineTextInput widget only fires the `on_change` event when the text values are changed on Windows. The MultilineTextInput in toga_winforms now uses `_text_history` to keep track of its text content, and the `on_change` event will only fire when the current text value differs from `_text_history`. 

It will prevent the MultilineTextInput widget from firing `on_change` events when only style changes to content have happened on Windows. This also resolves the issue that the MultilineTextInput widget fires the `on_change` event during its creation, as it needs to set the font size during its initialization process. 

Fix #3290

## Testing guide

For anyone wanting to test this - here's a step by step guide.

Create a positron-test directory:
```
mkdir positron-test
cd positron-test
```

Create and activate a Python3 virtual environment. If you're on Linux, this will need to be the system default python3 interpreter:
```
python3 -m venv venv
source ./venv/bin/activate
```

Clone the Toga repo, and checkout the code for this PR. I'm using the `gh` tool here; if you want to do it manually, you need the `fastapi` branch on my (@freakboy3742) fork of Toga:
```
git clone https://github.com/beeware/toga.git
cd toga
gh pr checkout 4156
cd ..
```

Install the checked-out version of Toga. If you're on macOS, use:
```
pip install -e ./toga/core -e ./toga/travertino -e ./toga/cocoa -e ./toga/positron
```
If you're on Linux, replace `./toga/cocoa` with `./toga/gtk`. On Window, use `./toga/winforms`.

Create a new Briefcase project:
```
briefcase new
```
Answer all the wizard questions with whatever answers you want. One of the last questions asks about your preferred GUI framework; select "Toga Positron (FastAPI server)" to test out the new option.

Then run the app. If you've accepted the default "Hello World" app name, this will be:
```
cd helloworld
briefcase dev
```
If you chose any other name, the `briefcase new` command should have shown you the commands to run.

pullRequest.title: Improve fallback handling for unloadable icons
pullRequest.body:
This PR improves icon error handling so unloadable icon files no longer crash app startup or icon construction.

Previously, `toga.Icon(...)` only treated `FileNotFoundError` as recoverable. If a candidate icon file existed but failed to decode/load in a backend (raising `ValueError`), the exception propagated and could crash the app.

This change makes `ValueError` recoverable in the same fallback flow:
- Regular icons: fallback to `toga.Icon.DEFAULT_ICON`.
- App icon (`Icon.APP_ICON`): keep existing fallback chain (`resources/<app>` -> bundled binary icon when bundled -> default icon).
- Warnings are logged when an icon is unloadable.

Tests were updated to assert fallback behavior (instead of expecting raised `ValueError`) for unloadable icons, including app icon cases.

Fixes #3565  
Fixes #3422

pullRequest.title: Standardize and clean up colors
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3611

- Previously, there was an `rgba` and an `hsla` class, and then `rgb` and `hsl` inherited from those, each locking their alpha in at opaque. In order to better align with CSS, now those parent classes are renamed to `rgb` and `hsl`, and the child classes are removed; `rgba` and `hsla` are simply direct aliases.
- Also in order to better match CSS, the constructors now silently clip (or in the case of hue, wrap) out-of-range values rather than throwing errors. They also convert them to consistent types: integers for red, blue, green, and hue; and floats for saturation, lightness, and alpha.
- `rgb` and `hsl`, in addition to their copy-pasteable-into-Python `__repr__`, gain a `__str__` that uses modern CSS syntax. For `rgb` this is simply a nice update, but for `hsl` it corrects [color rendering issues in web](https://github.com/beeware/toga/issues/3611#issuecomment-3108051814).
- Color objects are now read-only (in the sense that their `r`/`g`/`b`/`a` or `h`/`s`/`l`/`a` attributes are now properties with no setters). This also means that `rgb.rgb`/`hsl.hsl` can simply return itself, and `rgb.hsl`/`hsl.rgb` can cache its result after performing the calculation once.
- The string-parsing `color()` function now no longer attempts to handle `"rgb(...)"` / `"hsl(...)"` syntax; it still handles `#RGB` hex strings and named colors.

I could use some guidance on change notes for this. Should I include a removal note for the removed string-parsing, even though it was never documented? Should the rest of it be multiple change notes, or one big one with a bulleted list?

The test diffs are a mess to try and read. Basically, aside from adding tests for the functional changes, I:
- Grouped `rgb`/`rgba` and `hsl`/`hsla` tests together, removing anything clearly a duplicate;
- Removed duplication by saving and reusing parametrizations for multiple tests;
- Removed the `test_hsl`/`test_hsla` test in test_constructor.py, since it was a camouflaged color conversion test. Its test cases have now been rehomed to the test_color_conversions.py.

pullRequest.title: Android DetailedList: use theme text colors (replace R.color.black)
pullRequest.body: **Problem** On Android, DetailedList currently hard-codes R.color.black for row text. In dark mode, this makes text unreadable (black text on dark background). Fixes https://github.com/beeware/toga/issues/3747. **Solution** Replace the hard-coded color with theme-resolved colors: top_text: resolved from android.R.attr.textColorPrimary bottom_text: resolved from android.R.attr.textColorSecondary Fallback: Color.BLACK **Screenshots with solution implemented** As pointed out by [freakboy3742](https://github.com/freakboy3742) when the issue was raised, the decision whether to use textColorPrimary or textColorSecondary for bottom_text requires attention to android best practice. After looking at https://m3.material.io/components/lists/specs, I landed on textColorSecondary. top_text and bottom_text seem to work like a headline and supporting text, with varying emphases on color/contrast.

pullRequest.title: Correct failure winforms show/hide cursor test.
pullRequest.body:
At some point in the last week, the Winforms backend started failing a cursor show/hide test.

It appears that a recent Windows change means the `handle`-based check for determining cursor visibility no longer works. A cursor is *always* allocated; if you're in a touch-based environment (and CI evidently presents as "touch based"), there doesn't appear to be any API that can be used to determine visibility.

On that basis, I've modified the check so that in a "normal" cursor environment, the `flags`- based check is used; but if `flags` indicates the app is in a touch-based environment, the shadow state of the `_impl` is used.

Fixes #3783.

pullRequest.title: Centralize info for API documentation
pullRequest.body:
This is an attempt to consolidate some of the info for the API component documentation, so that there's less repetition, and thus less possibility of drift. It also fixes #3818 (changing all the title/links in the "APIs by platform" page to normal text and page links instead of code cross references) and makes a bit of progress toward #3819 by fixing some title case in the touched files.

As of https://github.com/beeware/beeware-docs-tools/pull/69, the BeeWare docs tools support the creation of custom macros in Python code. I've added a macros module, and defined macros in it for generating:

- The tables on the two API component reference pages (both the "main" one with descriptions, and the one with platform support)
- The repeated content at the top of each component's page:
  - Title
  - Description
  - Platform support (tabbed view for visual widgets, table for non-visual, and entirely blank for platform-agnostic things like sources)

There was already some drift between the descriptions on the index page, the individual pages, and the CSV; I mostly went with what was on the index, except for a few which were more fleshed out on the individual page. I've also put a period at the end of each, since this was inconsistent (and some *do* contain full sentences).

As John [pointed out](https://github.com/beeware/toga/issues/3818#issuecomment-3416136646), there was some drift in the supported platforms in the tabbed screenshot views too; now they pull directly from the CSV. They also all include automatically generated alt text, e.g. "MainWindow on macOS". ImageView was the sole component that used the same image file for multiple platforms; rather than make an exception in the code, I copied and renamed the file. I imagine we can live with an extra 30 KB... but let me know if you'd prefer the opposite approach.

I also fixed a broken link that was pointing to tutorial/tutorial-1.html. (Edit: not only did I accidentally not commit that, I'd missed a newer change to the same link and had to rebase to pull it in.) ~~Speaking of which, all the "<thing>.html" URLs from the old docs are broken with this overhaul. Perhaps [mkdocs-redirects](https://github.com/mkdocs/mkdocs-redirects) would be worth looking into... but this PR is already pretty big.~~ [Edit: handled in RTD settings]

Speaking of things that might still be good to do:
- I still think it would be good to combine the two API reference indices if possible — possibly with tabs, if feasible.
- I was able to simplify the gnarly CSS selectors for table styling a *little* bit by replacing the header's IDs with a class. It would be really nice, of course, to simply apply a class directly to the tables. Even though the list-attrs plugin doesn't support this, it looks like something workable might be possible using either [this `pymdownx.blocks.html` approach](https://github.com/squidfunk/mkdocs-material/discussions/6173#discussioncomment-7247726) or maybe even [this fork of attr-list](https://github.com/paulmelis/pm_markdown_extensions) (or applying its changes to the more current code).
- I renamed `widgets_by_platform.csv` to `apis_by_platform.csv`, since it's not just widgets (and to match the pages' actual title). I didn't change the markdown file's name though, or any others, to make them more consistent or in line with their titles. That can be a separate thing to (potentially) do, and appropriately redirect.

pullRequest.title: docs: normalize page titles and sidebar to sentence case (#3819)
pullRequest.body:
Issue #3819 documented that the docs sidebar and page titles were a mix of title case (e.g. `Release History`, `Success Stories`, `Topic Guides`) and sentence case. The maintainers' chosen approach in the issue thread:

> "All titles should be first-word-only capitalized, and the tutorial's sentence-like titles should be replaced with 'real' titles."

This PR does the full sweep: H1 titles, the matching sidebar entries, H2-H4 sub-headings, and tutorial page renames from sentence-like titles to descriptive short titles.

pullRequest.title: Color Improvements on Toga's Cocoa Backend
pullRequest.body:
<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->

For DateInput and TimeInput, adds background color implementations that invokes drawsBackground=True only with customized backgrounds; using drawsBackground=True draws the wrong background color when using the default background.

For several widgets, when using drawsBackground=False explicitly adds a clear color background, so that when you set a background color to red and then set to transparent, weird rendering (partially sometimes) retaining red background does not occur.

For several widgets, instead of using None as native color when color is reset, use the preset system color.  Sometimes this is defensive, sometimes this is functional (see e.g. TextInput foreground)

For Selection, background color looked weird, and was documented as impossible; I explicitly made it so, bypassing default problematic color implementaitons.

Corresponding probe modifictionas included.

Fixes #3919 based on @YapWC's work at #4073.

pullRequest.title: Ensure app name is normalised, and comes from an explicit source
pullRequest.body:
Makes 2 small changes to the determination of app name:

* If an App ID is provided (which is documented as required unless there's Briefcase-style packaging metadata), the app name is determined from the app ID, rather than the module name
* The app ID is *always* normalised.

This fixes #3926, which identified that running `python -m toga_demo` resulted in the wrong app icon (because the app icon was evaluated as `resources/toga_demo`, whereas if you ran with `briefcase`, you would get the icon (because Briefcase would embed the icon into the app binary)

It also fixes the fact that `toga` wasn't listed as a dependency (because of the use of setuptools-dynamic-dependencies), and that some of the other definitions needed for successful Briefcase operation weren't listed.

The app won't run on iOS, Android or Web, so those configurations are now disabled.

pullRequest.title: Rearrange / rename API reference docs
pullRequest.body: Fixes #3941 ## Changes - Rearranges the API reference docs as listed in https://github.com/beeware/toga/issues/3941#issuecomment-3688146180 - Also moves the Architecture page from /reference/internals/architecture to /topics/architecture - Adds redirects for all moves, using [mkdocs-redirects](https://github.com/mkdocs/mkdocs-redirects) - Excludes snippets from sitemap, using [mkdocs-exclude](https://github.com/apenwarr/mkdocs-exclude) - Unfortunately there doesn't seem to be a way of excluding the nav file, SUMMARY.md, without breaking literate-nav and getting just the default auto-generated navigation. It's not a file *meant* for public consumption, but I don't suppose it does any harm for it to be listed... I've checked the redirects by downloading the 0.5.3 sitemap and using the following script: ```python import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET import httpx from rich import print tree = ET.parse("053 sitemap.xml") root = tree.getroot() for child in root: path = child[0].text.removeprefix("https://toga.beeware.org/") status = httpx.head(f"http://127.0.0.1:8000/{path}").status_code color = "green" if status == 200 else "red" print(f"{path}: [{color}]{status}[/{color}]") ``` I also turned on traffic analytics on readthedocs, so I could [see what's been getting 404 errors](https://app.readthedocs.org/dashboard/toga/traffic-analytics/) and add those too. ## Remaining questions - Whether Document goes in Resources or Application Components. - Finalizing the shortened API descriptions, and/or keeping both a long and a short version. Now that I've tried it out, I think sticking to one consistent (brief) description is still the way to go; it reads more like the top line of a docstring. This may necessitate reintroducing some of the removed detail at the beginning of the Usage section. I've waited to do this, or to spend much time going over and finalizing shortened versions, before getting feedback on whether the general approach looks good to others. - How (or if) to visually make the Container Widgets table be visually a subcategory of Widgets. I've tried using a lower-level heading: And even mucking about with a faux-header insert: I don't think either of these look good or read as intended, and the latter exacerbates how tall the Widgets table already is, requiring even more scrolling up to find the header labels. (Unfortunately, sticky headers [doesn't seem to be possible](https://github.com/squidfunk/mkdocs-material/issues/4347) with mkdocs Materials.) It's already structurally a subcategory, and reads as such in the sidebar nav. pullRequest.title: Refactor `Table` and `Tree` to use `Column` objects internally
pullRequest.body:
This implements the first phase discussed in #4041 - a refactor of `Table` and `Tree` that pulls common logic into a `Column` object. This is a purely implementation detail at present, the `Column` objects are not exposed to the users in any way.

In particular, all current tests should pass unmodified.

Internally the Tree and Table have grown three new attributes:
- `_columns`: the list of column objects
- `_show_headers`: a bool that is true when no headings were passed
- `_data_accessors`: these are the accessors used when creating an ad-hoc `ListSource` from lists or dicts. The behaviour of accessors on column changes is a bit unclear, but looking at the code you can have more accessors than just those used in the headers. In future it might be good to make the behaviour a bit more specific, particularly around adding/removing columns (eg. should a completely new accessor be added to the list, where should it be added, and what does that do to existing data sources? Similarly for removal.).

This is at a state where it can be reviewed as a purely internal refactor. We probably want to expose some of these changes as part of the public API, but given that there is some complexity in this PR it's probably better to make those changes in a separate PR.

Ref #4041.
Fixes #4071.

pullRequest.title: Add `Column` objects to `Tree` and `Table` public API
pullRequest.body:
This is an initial cut at making `Column` objects part of the public API for `Tree` and `Table` objects.

This PR:
- adapts the `Tree` and `Table` APIs to use `ColumnT` protocol objects in the place of column headings and accessors.
- adds a read-only `columns` property with the current columns
- preserves backwards compatibility for the old API
- adds a `show_headings` argument gives more direct control over whether table headers are shown
- makes some changes to the internal `Tree` and `Table` implementation API, passing a column object rather than heading and accessor (and keeps backwards compatibility with any 3rd-party implementations)
- adds a basic `Column` ABC which is more appropriate for creating simple column subclasses than `AccessorColumn`
- adds equality tests and hashability for `AccessorColumn`.
- documentation for Columns
- an example showing different ways to use columns

Fixes #4091.

pullRequest.title: Correct iOS testbed failures
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #4103.

Slightly modifies the `wait_for_window` assertion so that it waits for the layout property that has been reported by #4103 to be true. 

Historically, `wait_for_window` has been a near no-op (in fast mode, it's a one cycle event loop tick unless you ask for a specific window state to be present). The changes to adjust for window resizes in #4043 made window resizing slightly more complicated, and added an additional assertion on layout; my theory is that the failures have been caused because 1 event loop may not be sufficient on slower machines for the full layout to occur.

This change adds an "up to 5s" wait for the problem assertion as a pre-condition for `wait_for_window()`.

It also bumps the testbed timeout to 20 minutes.

pullRequest.title: Add support for column resizing in Qt tables
pullRequest.body:
## Summary
- switch Qt table headers to `Interactive` mode with a minimum section size so users can resize columns
- keep equal auto-fit sizing until a user manually resizes, then preserve manual widths across source changes and layout updates
- reset auto-fit when columns are inserted/removed so new column layouts remain sane
- add a capability-gated testbed test for column resizing and expose resize support in the Qt table probe
- add a changelog note for #4109

Closes #4109.

- `uv run --python 3.12 --with ruff ruff check qt/src/toga_qt/widgets/table.py qt/tests_backend/widgets/table.py testbed/tests/widgets/test_table.py`

- `QT_QPA_PLATFORM=offscreen uv run --python 3.12 --with ./travertino --with ./core --with ./qt --with PySide6-Essentials~=6.10.0 python` script to verify:
  - manual column resize works
  - manual width persists after `change_source()`

pullRequest.title: Harden the definition for icons in column data.
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #4135 

Previously, the specification for icons in table data was documented as a 2-tuple. However, on Windows, n-tuples were accepted; on Cocoa; any 2-element collections were accepted. 

The recent changes to introduce a Column type in #4042 removed the inconsistencies, but allowed for 2-element collections. 

This PR hardens the specification for Column to *only* accept tuples.

pullRequest.title: Use adb command from $ANDROID_HOME
pullRequest.body:
* Closes #4210 

This was caused by #4168, which implemented testbed data uploading on Android using a copy of `adb` installed with `apt`. Ubuntu doesn't keep old versions of packages in its repository for very long, so every `apt install` in a script must be preceded by `apt update`. But there was no need to use `apt` at all in this case, because there was already a copy of `adb` in `$ANDROID_HOME`.

Also removed the redundant `pre-copy-command` variable, as the `copy-command` is quite capable of containing multiple commands.

pullRequest.title: Optional accessors in Sources
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #4221

This PR updates ListSource and TreeSource so accessors are optional when the input data is mapping-based (for example, dictionaries). Previously, accessors were effectively required for all non-Row inputs, which made mapping-based usage more restrictive than necessary.

Changes:
* Accepts accessors as optional (None defaults to an empty accessor list).
* Preserves strict validation for non-mapping data, which still requires explicit accessors.
* Applies the same behavior in both list and tree sources.
* Add test for valid and invalid scenarios.

pullRequest.title: Bug fix / Bug: When putting multiple windows into presentation mode, only last one succeeded
pullRequest.body:
<!--- If this PR relates to an issue, include Refs #XXX or Fixes #XXX -->
Fixes #4233

<!--- What problem does this change solve? -->
When `enter_presentation_mode` is called with multiple windows, each desktop backend's `set_window_state` guard checks whether any other window is already in `PRESENTATION` mode and calls `exit_presentation_mode()` if so. This means the second window's `set_window_state` call kicks the first window out of presentation, leaving only the last window in presentation mode.

<!--- Describe your changes in detail -->
### Changes

**Smarten the guard in all four desktop backends** (cocoa, gtk, qt, winforms): Add `state != WindowState.PRESENTATION` to the existing guard condition so it doesn't fire when entering presentation mode for another window. The guard still correctly exits presentation when a window's state is changed to something else (e.g., `FULLSCREEN`, `MINIMIZED`).

**Add `exit_presentation_mode()` at the top of `enter_presentation_mode`** in core: Since the smarter guard no longer exits presentation when entering presentation for new windows, the core now explicitly exits any existing presentation mode before entering with a new set of windows. This preserves the non-cumulative semantics: `enter_presentation_mode([w1])` followed by `enter_presentation_mode([w2])` correctly exits `w1`.

**Add a recursion guard to `exit_presentation_mode`**: With multiple windows in presentation mode, exiting one window's presentation triggers the backend guard (which sees other windows still in presentation), causing `exit_presentation_mode` to be called recursively. A simple flag prevents this.

### Design consideration

I initially considered consolidating the guard logic into the core layer (e.g., in the `Window.state` property setter) to avoid duplicating it across all four backends. However, the backends have complex state transition machinery that the guard interacts with, and the scope of that refactor felt disproportionate to the bug fix. This PR takes the minimal, targeted approach of fixing the guard in place and leaves a potential refactor for a separate effort.

### Pre-existing screen behavior

During testing I observed that windows don't always return to their original screen after exiting presentation mode. The cocoa backend does save/restore the screen via `_before_presentation_mode_screen`, but the restoration doesn't appear to work reliably. Additionally, when passing a list to `enter_presentation_mode`, windows are assigned to screens by list order (`zip(windows, self.screens)`) rather than by their current screen, so they may end up on unexpected screens. I did not attempt to fix these issues in this PR.

### Manual test scripts

All four scenarios from the issue discussion were tested manually on macOS with two monitors:

**Case 1: `enter_presentation_mode([w1, w2])` — both should be in presentation**
```python
import toga


class TestApp(toga.App):
    def startup(self):
        self.main_window = toga.MainWindow()
        self.main_window.content = toga.Box(children=[toga.Label("Main Window")])
        self.main_window.show()

        self.other_window = toga.Window()
        self.other_window.content = toga.Box(
            children=[
                toga.Label("Other Window"),
                toga.Button("Toggle", on_press=self.toggle),
            ]
        )
        self.other_window.show()

    def toggle(self, widget, **kwargs):
        if self.in_presentation_mode:
            self.exit_presentation_mode()
        else:
            self.enter_presentation_mode([self.main_window, self.other_window])


def main():
    return TestApp("Test App", "org.beeware.test")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main().main_loop()
```

**Case 2: `enter_presentation_mode([w1])` then `enter_presentation_mode([w2])` — w1 should exit**
```python
"""Case 2: enter_presentation_mode([w1]) then enter_presentation_mode([w2]) -> w1 should exit."""
import toga


class TestApp(toga.App):
    def startup(self):
        self.main_window = toga.MainWindow()
        self.main_window.content = toga.Box(
            children=[
                toga.Label("Main Window (w1)"),
                toga.Button("Step 1: Present w1 only", on_press=self.step1),
                toga.Button("Step 2: Present w2 only", on_press=self.step2),
                toga.Button("Exit presentation", on_press=self.exit_preso),
            ]
        )
        self.main_window.show()

        self.other_window = toga.Window(title="Other Window")
        self.other_window.content = toga.Box(
            children=[
                toga.Label("Other Window (w2)"),
                toga.Button("Step 1: Present w1 only", on_press=self.step1),
                toga.Button("Step 2: Present w2 only", on_press=self.step2),
                toga.Button("Exit presentation", on_press=self.exit_preso),
            ]
        )
        self.other_window.show()

    def step1(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Step 1: entering presentation mode with [main_window]")
        self.enter_presentation_mode([self.main_window])
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")

    def step2(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Step 2: entering presentation mode with [other_window]")
        self.enter_presentation_mode([self.other_window])
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")

    def exit_preso(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Exiting presentation mode")
        self.exit_presentation_mode()
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")


def main():
    return TestApp("Test Case 2", "org.beeware.test2")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main().main_loop()
```

**Case 3: `enter_presentation_mode([w1, w2])` then `enter_presentation_mode([w2])` — w1 exits, w2 stays**
```python
"""Case 3: enter_presentation_mode([w1, w2]) then enter_presentation_mode([w2])
-> w1 should exit, w2 should remain in presentation."""
import toga


class TestApp(toga.App):
    def startup(self):
        self.main_window = toga.MainWindow()
        self.main_window.content = toga.Box(
            children=[
                toga.Label("Main Window (w1)"),

                toga.Button("Step 2: Present [w2] only", on_press=self.step2),
                toga.Button("Exit presentation", on_press=self.exit_preso),
            ]

        self.main_window.show()

        self.other_window = toga.Window(title="Other Window")
        self.other_window.content = toga.Box(
            children=[
                toga.Label("Other Window (w2)"),
                toga.Button("Step 1: Present [w1, w2]", on_press=self.step1),
                toga.Button("Step 2: Present [w2] only", on_press=self.step2),
                toga.Button("Exit presentation", on_press=self.exit_preso),
            ]
        )
        self.other_window.show()

    def step1(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Step 1: entering presentation mode with [main_window, other_window]")
        self.enter_presentation_mode([self.main_window, self.other_window])
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")

    def step2(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Step 2: entering presentation mode with [other_window]")
        self.enter_presentation_mode([self.other_window])
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")

    def exit_preso(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Exiting presentation mode")
        self.exit_presentation_mode()
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")


def main():
    return TestApp("Test Case 3", "org.beeware.test3")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main().main_loop()
```

**Case 4: `enter_presentation_mode([w1])` then `enter_presentation_mode([w2, w1])` — w1 moves screens**
```python
"""Case 4: enter_presentation_mode([w1]) then enter_presentation_mode([w2, w1])
-> w1 should move to the other screen, w2 takes w1's old screen."""
import toga


class TestApp(toga.App):
    def startup(self):
        self.main_window = toga.MainWindow()
        self.main_window.content = toga.Box(
            children=[
                toga.Label("Main Window (w1)"),
                toga.Button("Step 1: Present [w1]", on_press=self.step1),
                toga.Button("Step 2: Present [w2, w1]", on_press=self.step2),
                toga.Button("Exit presentation", on_press=self.exit_preso),
            ]
        )
        self.main_window.show()

        self.other_window = toga.Window(title="Other Window")
        self.other_window.content = toga.Box(
            children=[
                toga.Label("Other Window (w2)"),
                toga.Button("Step 1: Present [w1]", on_press=self.step1),
                toga.Button("Step 2: Present [w2, w1]", on_press=self.step2),
                toga.Button("Exit presentation", on_press=self.exit_preso),
            ]
        )
        self.other_window.show()

    def step1(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Step 1: entering presentation mode with [main_window]")
        self.enter_presentation_mode([self.main_window])
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  main_window.screen = {self.main_window.screen}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.screen = {self.other_window.screen}")

    def step2(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Step 2: entering presentation mode with [other_window, main_window]")
        self.enter_presentation_mode([self.other_window, self.main_window])
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  main_window.screen = {self.main_window.screen}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.screen = {self.other_window.screen}")

    def exit_preso(self, widget, **kwargs):
        print("Exiting presentation mode")
        self.exit_presentation_mode()
        print(f"  main_window.state = {self.main_window.state}")
        print(f"  main_window.screen = {self.main_window.screen}")
        print(f"  other_window.state = {self.other_window.state}")
        print(f"  other_window.screen = {self.other_window.screen}")


def main():
    return TestApp("Test Case 4", "org.beeware.test4")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main().main_loop()

pullRequest.title: Work around Cocoa multiline label layout bug.
pullRequest.body:
If a multi-line label contains an empty line, and the label is right aligned, Cocoa incorrectly evaluates the intrinsic width of the label as "-1" (or cannot compute). 

Work around this by temporarily switching text alignment to LEFT before computing intrinsic size.

Fixes #4315.

pullRequest.title: docs(tutorial): fix Tutorial 4 numbered list rendering
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Closes #4333. Fixes the rendering bug at https://toga.beeware.org/en/latest/tutorial/tutorial-4/ where the two-item ordered list at the top of the page rendered as `1.` / `1.` instead of `1.` / `2.`.

## Why this matters

The HTML comments `<!-- rumdl-disable MD013 -->` and `<!-- rumdl-enable MD013 -->` inside the list block split it into two single-item lists in the mkdocs/Material output, so the second item restarted at `1.`.

A bonus: those comments are also redundant. `pyproject.toml:111` sets `[tool.rumdl.MD013] line_length = 999999`, which already effectively disables MD013 globally. There's nothing for the disable/enable pair to gate. So the fix is just to delete them, which both unbreaks the list and removes a bit of noise.

## Diff

```
@@ -4,7 +4,6 @@
 ...
-<!-- rumdl-disable MD013 -->
 1. We first define the drawing operations we want to perform in a new function:
@@ -15,7 +14,6 @@
     ```
     Notice that we also created and used a new fill state called `eye_whites`. ...
-<!-- rumdl-enable MD013 -->

 2. Next we create a new Canvas:
```

Plus a `changes/4333.doc.md` change-fragment per `[tool.towncrier]` (`type = [..., { directory = "doc", ... }]` in pyproject.toml).

## Verification

After the change, the list reads:

```
1. We first define the drawing operations ...
   <code block + paragraph>

2. Next we create a new Canvas:
   <code block>
```

mkdocs renders this as a continuous ordered list, no comment-induced split.
